coredata.dc:title: Integration of cadmium sulfide quantum dots with calcium alginate microbeads for dual-functional mercury (II) ion sensing and removal

coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Ambika
Jaswal, Nancy
Monika,
Dutta, R.K.
Kumar, Pramod
coredata.dc:description: Mercury contamination poses human health as well as ecosystem at significant risks. This study introduces a novel approach for the selective detection with removal of Hg2+ ions from aqueous media by developing a dual functional adsorbent that combines calcium alginate microbeads (FMCAB) with capped cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs). CdS QDs immobilized on calcium alginate microbeads in magnetite facilitated efficient separation from the adsorption system under an external magnetic field. The fluorescence properties of CdS QDs remain largely unaffected before and after adsorption of Hg2+ ions on FMCAB, enabling selective monitoring of these ions. The adsorbent demonstrated a high adsorption capacity of around 160 μg/g and exhibited a quick and effective process. FMCAB evidenced selectivity over the other metal ions with limits of detection 12.9 μM. The results from this research pave the way for novel applications of FMCAB for heavy metal ions sensing probes and its removal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FMCAB
CdS QDs
Mercury contamination
Metal ions sensing
Removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent magnetic calcium alginate beads (FMCAB)
Fluorescence quenching due to adsorption of Hg2+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and emission spectrum of CdS QDs,.
HRTEM image of CdS QDs (inset showing lattice fringes with interplaner distance).
showing the XRD pattern of CdS QDs, FMCAB and IO NPs.
M-H curve for (a) IONPs and (b) FMCAB.
FESEM images of FMCAB at different magnification with EDAX spectrum.
Plot exhibiting effect of adsorbent dose on Qe and % adsorption.
Selectivity study of FMCAB towards toxic heavy metal ions.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of FMCAB treated with different Hg (II) concentration (C0 = 1 mg/L to C0 = 50 mg/L) were recorded with excitation wavelength λex = 370 nm and 1 nm slit width.

Stern Volmer Plot between Io-I/Io and Mercury (II) Concentration [Io and I represents the fluorescence intensity of FMCAB in absence and presence of Hg2+ ions, respectively] (Inset shows the corresponding linear calibration curve).
Graph showing the reusability of FMCAB.
Langmuir isotherm model for mercury ion adsorption on FMCAB.
Comparison of as prepared sample with the reported studies.
Thermodynamic parameters for the sorption of Hg2+ onto CA-IO- CdS microbeads.

head.ce:title: Integration of cadmium sulfide quantum dots with calcium alginate microbeads for dual-functional mercury (II) ion sensing and removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Bhagalpur National College, Bhagalpur (T. M. B. U. Bhagalpur), Bihar 812007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Bhagalpur National College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Bhagalpur National College Bhagalpur (T. M. B. U. Bhagalpur), Bihar, 812007- India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Sciences, Central University of Himachal Pradesh, Academic Block, Shahpur, District, Kangra, H.P. 176206, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Sciences, Central University of Himachal Pradesh, Academic Block

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee 247667, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, 247667-India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury contamination poses human health as well as ecosystem at significant risks. This study introduces a novel approach for the selective detection with removal of Hg2+ ions from aqueous media by developing a dual functional adsorbent that combines calcium alginate microbeads (FMCAB) with capped cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs). CdS QDs immobilized on calcium alginate microbeads in magnetite facilitated efficient separation from the adsorption system under an external magnetic field. The fluorescence properties of CdS QDs remain largely unaffected before and after adsorption of Hg2+ ions on FMCAB, enabling selective monitoring of these ions. The adsorbent demonstrated a high adsorption capacity of around 160 μg/g and exhibited a quick and effective process. FMCAB evidenced selectivity over the other metal ions with limits of detection 12.9 μM. The results from this research pave the way for novel applications of FMCAB for heavy metal ions sensing probes and its removal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) is considered as a highly toxic heavy metal present in the environment [1]. Apart from the natural sources of mercury exposure to the environment, various anthropogenic sources cause elevation in the mercury level, especially in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystem [2]. Anthropogenic activity mainly includes electricity generation and various industrial productions, such as chlor-alkali, plastics, paint, dye, metal finishing, electroplating, photography etc [3]. Owing to the volatility, bioaccumulation and persistency, mercury can affect the health of human beings [4]. Mercury can pose threat not only to human health, but also have detrimental effects for the aquatic ecosystem. Monomethyl mercury is the most dangerous species, but in aquatic system, this metal is generally present as complex of divalent cation Hg (II) [5]. It can cause poisoning at high concentration (i.e., parts per million levels of mercury), and also even at low concentration (i.e., parts per billion levels of mercury). The maximum uptake limit recommended by World Health Organization (WHO) is 0.3 mg/week and 0.001 mg/L in drinking water [6]. The well-known health hazards due to the toxicity of mercury at Minamata, Japan and Iraq are well known [7,8]. The toxicological effects of mercury depend upon the type of mercury compound, the mode of entry into the body and the amount ingested. It is mutagenic, carcinogenic, teratogenic and promotes tyrosinemia. It also causes, mental and emotional deterioration, loss of consciousness, involuntary mobilization, blindness, etc. Due to potential health risk to human health, the mercury contaminated waste water have attracted a global concern, leading to a growing demand to develop a suitable mercury remediation technique, especially from wastewater [9].

Various primary methods and techniques have been employed regarding mercury removal from water, these are ion exchange [10], chemical precipitation [11], coagulation [12], bioadsorption/biological methods [13], cementation [14], reverse osmosis [15]. Precipitation methods generally result in large volumes of mercury containing sludge, whereas ion exchange is effective for wastes with low dissolved metal concentrations. Moreover, due to non-favorable specific nature of industrial effluents (low pH, coexistence of varieties of other metal cations and anions, oil emulsion, particles etc.), the effective and selective removal of mercury ions from waste water is a difficult and costly method [16]. Separation through adsorption is found to be one of the most facile, convenient and practical technology among the existing Hg remediation technologies [17].

Wide range of nanoscale materials as adsorbents explored for removing higher concentration of Hg2+ in aqueous medium. Some of these studies reported large Qe values ranging between 15 and 30 [18,19], taking into consideration of high initial concentration of Hg2+. Such high Qe value is attributable to large surface area of nanomaterials, owing to their small sizes. Major problems in these protocols are separation of adsorbent from the reaction medium, mass loss during separation, low mechanical strength and subsequent reuse of the adsorbent. In view of this, subnanoscale materials with excellent adsorption properties can be thought to be immobilized on larger sized substrates, so that post separation adsorption is easy. Similar strategy are employed for various heavy metal removal by immobilizing Metal Oxides, Metal Sulphides, Graphene Oxides on suitable polymers. Carbon dots have been emerged as a promising material for detection in various fields such as bioimaging and environmental analysis as they have unique characteristics including high emission quantum yields, controllable size, water solubility and photostability [20]. Carbon quantum dots based water soluble systems have been derived for detecting Hg2+ ions because of their strong fluorescence signal which has been quenched by Hg2+ ions through electron transfer [21]. Carbon quantum dots can also act as reducing and stabilizing agents to develop a water soluble composite Ag/CQDs for Hg2+ ions detection, leads to a dual signal strategy [22]. Ion-specific selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of mercury ions have drawn attention to polymer- based systems with stimuli responsive properties [23]. A very promising material, which offers such criteria, is alginate, a natural anionic polymer. Alginate is a linear polysaccharide distributed widely in the cell wall of brown algae. It is a copolymer of α-L-guluronate (G) and β-D-mannuronate. The capability of this copolymer to form stable biodegradable gels in the presence of divalent cation, makes its usability in the field of adsorption. Moreover, alginate matrix has been mostly used as the matrix for the immobilization of various microbial cells or nanomaterials [24]. In the presence of various anionic functional groups (OH- and COOH-) this polymer can also bind metal ions strongly, so immobilization of functionalized nanomaterials with this polymer can promote the adsorptive capacity of the adsorbent [25]. The limitations of traditional polymers are overcome by these water-soluble systems, which help with environmental monitoring and remediation by effectively detecting mercury ions in real water samples or sewage through mechanisms like electron transfer and specific group interactions [26].

Recent advancements in fluorescent probes and nanocomposites have significantly improved the selective detection and removal of mercury ions (Hg2+) in aqueous environments, exhibited in Table 1. These materials, such as fluorescein-phenylalaninol (FPA) conjugates, carboxymethyl chitosan-functionalized magnetic-fluorescent nanocomposites, and tryptophan-dithiocarbamate-based polymeric probes, offer both high sensitivity and specificity. The FPA conjugates show dual visual and fluorescent responses, while the chitosan-functionalized nanocomposites combine magnetic separation with strong photoluminescence. These materials exhibit high removal efficiencies, making them versatile tools for mercury sensing and remediation in complex matrices [27–29].

However, fluorescence-based techniques offer distinct advantages over methods like colorimetric or inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis. They provide highly sensitive, non-destructive, and real-time monitoring capabilities, facilitating quick and accurate detection without the need for complex instrumentation. These attributes make fluorescence a superior method for environmental monitoring and contamination control.h

In the present study, we exhibit an approach for developing a magnetic fluorescent adsorbent for selective removal of Hg2+ ions from aqueous medium, where Mercapto acetic acid (MAA) capped cadmium sulphide quantum dots (CdS QDs) were successfully immobilized on the surface of magnetite (iron oxide nanoparticles) entrapped calcium alginate microbeads. The use of iron oxide nanoparticles (IO NPs) facilitates our material for effective separation from the adsorption system, by using external magnetic field. Moreover, the proposed magnetic separation method does not allow further contamination by other separation techniques. The fluorescent properties of CdS QDs were not much affected in the presence of polymer matrix and IONPs. Thus, with the help of FMCAB, we can selectively monitor the presence of Hg2+ ions by comparing the optical properties of FMCAB before and after adsorption.

Sodium alginate powder, calcium chloride (98 %, w/v), cadmium chloride (98 %, w/v), mercaptoacetic acid (80 %, v/v), sodium sulphide (58–62 %, w/v), mercuric chloride (99.5 %, w/v), toluene (99 %, v/v), sodium hydroxide (98 %, w/v), what is w/v, hydrochloric acid (35 %, v/v), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (5 %, w/v), ferrous sulphate heptahydrate (5 %, w/v), ammonia (25 %,v/v), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) di-sodium salt, thiourea, and acetone were procured from Himedia Pvt. Ltd India. The commercial ICP-MS standard solution of metal ions, e.g., Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni 2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Pd2+ and Mg2+ were purchased from Merck, India. All chemicals used in this study were not subjected to any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent magnetic calcium alginate beads (FMCAB)
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of FMCAB consists of three steps; firstly, iron oxide nanoparticles were prepared by precipitation method at room temperature using ferric and ferrous salts in the ratio 2:1 as the precursors. The pH was maintained at 10 with the help of ammonia solution [Saurabh Sahu]. The black coloured iron oxide nanoparticles were collected at the bottom of the beaker and separated by using magnetic separation method followed by washing several times with a solution of water and ethanol. The iron oxide nanoparticles were dried through lyophilisation and used for further studies. Secondly, CdS quantum dots were synthesized as suggested in the literature [19]. Briefly, 0.05 M CdCl2 solution was mixed with 0.04 M thioglycolic acid (also referred as mercaptoacetic acid) prepared in toluene. The CdS quantum dots were prepared by adding freshly prepared 0.05 M Na2S drop wise while maintain the reaction medium at pH 8 using NaOH solution and kept under constant stirring for 3 h till the solution turned pale green. A fraction of the pale green solution comprising CdS quantum dots was subjected to precipitation by adding acetone. The obtained precipitate was washed five times in deionised Millipore water (Ω = 18 mega Ohm) and lyophilized to obtain powdered samples of CdS which was used for characterization. Finally, in the third step, 1.5 % (w/v) sodium alginate powder was added in the homogenised aqueous solution of CdS QDs and mixed properly by using magnetic stirrer. A known amount of synthesized iron oxide nanoparticles re-dispersed in deionised water was added to the above mixture solution and stirred vigorously for 3 h. This mixture was taken in a burette and was added drop wise into a dilute CaCl2 solution (2.3 % w/v) through a micro-tip nozzle attached to the burette to form smaller spherical beads. These microbeads were then kept in freezer at 4 °C for next 24 h to impart rigidity. These microbeads were washed several times using deionised water to remove impurities and kept in oven at 40 °C for overnight drying. The concentrations of CdS QDs and IONPs used in the FMCAB synthesis were determined to be 11.75 % w/w and 5.88 % w/w respectively.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of CdS QDs was recorded in the wavelength ranging between 200 and 800 nm using Shimadzu, UV-1800. Corresponding fluorescence emission spectrum of CdS QDs and FMCAB were recorded using HORIBA FluorEssence Spectrometer with an excitation wavelength (λex) of 370 nm. Further, powdered nanomaterials and dried FMCAB were characterised under Bruker ARS D8 Advance diffractometer using graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the 2θ range between 20° to 80° at a scanning rate of 2° min−1. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images were captured with Zeiss Ultra plus based ultra-55, at an operating voltage of 20 kV. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX) hyphenated with the FESEM determines the elemental composition of materials. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CdS QDs were recorded with FEI Technai-G2 microscope, operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The samples for TEM analysis were prepared by placing a drop of MAA capped CdS QDs dispersed in water on a 150-mesh carbon coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. The morphology of the as synthesized FMCAB was also determined by optical microscope (Zeiss, Axiovert 25, Germany). The magnetic property of the iron oxide nanoparticles and FMCAB was studied with the help of vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Princeton Applied Research Model, PAR 155). A known amount of lyophilized sample was packed in a diamagnetic capsule and was inserted in a polyethylene straw as a sample holder. The magnetization measurements using VSM were recorded from the hysteresis loop of M-H curve in the range ± 1000 Oe at room temperature. The quantitative metal analysis during different adsorption batches were estimated by the air-acetylene method using Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (AAS, Perkin Elmer) and inductively coupled Plasma- Mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) (Perkin Elmer Sciex, equipped with ELAN DRC-e facility) safter suitable calibration.

Solutions of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were prepared by sequential dilution of stock solution (1000 mg/L) in acetate buffer solution. Firstly, pH was optimized by conducting adsorption study at pH ranging between 3 and 7. The optimizations of the parameters namely, adsorbent dose (2.5 g/L to 12.5 g/L), contact time (2 min to 90 min), analyte concentration (105 mg/L to 205 mg/L) and effect of temperature (288 K to 318 K) were performed at the optimized pH of the Hg2+ ions test solution. All the batches of adsorption studies were carried using 250 mL of Hg2+ ions test solution (105 mg/L). Subsequently, the aliquot were then analysed using Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) equipped with Gas Discharged Lamp (GDL). All experiments were carried in triplicate and the average values were reported here.

Selectivity study for Hg2+ ions on functionalized FMCAB adsorbent was studied using multielemental standard solution used for ICP analysis, where concentration of each metal ion was 1 mg/L. To find out the adsorption profile of Hg2+ ion in the presence of selected cations namely, Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni 2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Pd2+ and Mg2+ metal mixture prepared at optimized pH were kept in contact with FMCAB. Further, the role of CdS QDs in the fabricated FMCAB towards adsorption of Hg2+ ions was assessed by comparing the adsorption of these metal ions in the presence of adsorbent comprised with and without CdS QDs. All the adsorption parameters were same as that of Hg2+ adsorption studies.

To ensure the practical usability and cost effective application of the adsorbent, desorption and recycling of the adsorbed Hg2+ FMCAB was studied in a batch system. Various eluting agents for Hg2+ ions e.g., water, 2 M HCl, 0.1 M HNO3, 0.1 M EDTA, 0.1 M CH3COONa and acidic thiourea were used. For desorption-adsorption cycle, 50 mL of 105 mg/L Hg2+ solution at pH = 6 was equilibrated with optimized adsorbent dose for 30 min. These Hg2+ loaded FMCAB were magnetically collected and washed twice with deionised water to remove unadsorbed Hg2+ and suspended into homogeneous mixture of 2 M HCl and 2 % (w/v) thiourea for next 30 min and the supenatant was collected to analyse desorbed Hg2+ concentration by atomic absorption spectroscopy. Hg2+ unloaded FMCAB was conditioned at pH 6 and again placed into 105 mg/L concentration of Hg2+ solution for next adsoption process (30 min). A complete adsorption-desorption cycle was repeated 5 times without changing adsorbent. After each adsorption or desorption, aliquote was collected and its concentration were determined by AAS.

Hg2+ concentration ranging between 1 mg/L and 100 mg/L was prepared with serial dilution of stock solution (i.e., 1000 mg/L) in acetate buffer medium at pH 6. Further, 0.15 g of fluorescent microbeads (7.5 g/L) were immersed in 20 mL of each test solution and equilibrated for 30 min. Hg2+ ions adsorbed FMCABs were taken out from solution and dried at tissue paper. The fluorescence emission spectra of these beads were recorded with excitation wavelength 370 nm and 1 nm slit width.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of MAA capped CdS QDs revealed maximum absorbance at a wavelength of (λmax) 356 nm ( Fig. 1a). A tentative size of QDs was calculated to be 2.21 nm using the empirical formula.
D (nm) = (-6.6521 ×10−8)λ3 + (1.9557 ×10−4)λ2 – (9.2352 ×10−2)λ + 13.29

Where λ = wavelength at maximum absorbance. The determined particle size was in good agreement with those measured by TEM, revealing particles of sizes less than 3 nm in diameter. The crystalline nature of synthesized QDs was reflected from the observation of lattice fringes with inter-planar distance of 0.31 nm ( Fig. 2), which is less than that reported by us for well crystalline CdS quantum dots prepared by a different protocol [30].The fluorescence emission spectrum of CdS QDs obtained by exciting at λ = 370 nm, revealed broad and strong emission profile centering at a peak corresponding to λ = 491 nm (Fig. 1b). The broadness in the emission spectrum is attributable to trap state emission arising from surface trap states of CdS nano crystallites.

The XRD analysis of IONPs revealed the cubic structure from the position and relative intensities of observed Bragg’s diffraction pattern at the planes (220), (311), 400), (511) and (400) ( Fig. 3), which matched with JCPDS 01–11111 data. The average crystallite size (D) of the IONPs was determined to be about 8 nm, which was calculated with the help of Debye-Scherrer formula, i.e., D = 0.9λ/Δcosθ, where λ is the X-ray wavelength, Δ is the line broadening at half-height of the most intense peak (i.e., 311) and θ is the Brag angle of the particle. The magnetic property of the IONPs was confirmed from the VSM measurement and the concerned M-H plot revealed a saturation magnetization of 60 emu/g ( Fig. 4a). The M-H plot also revealed negligible coercivity and remanence magnetization which are characteristic of superparamagnetic property of the iron oxide nanoparticles [31].

The morphology of FMCABs in dry condition was studied by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) which revealed nearly spherical shaped structures with diameter of about 500 μm ( Fig. 5a). At higher resolution the surface of the beads were found to be decorated with nanostructures (Fig. 5b) whose elemental composition consisted of characteristic x-ray peaks of Cd, S, Fe and O (Fig. 5c). The observed elemental composition of FMCAB is in favour of the presence of CdS QDs and IONPs at the surface of the microbeads. The magnetic property of the FMCAB was corroborated from the VSM measurement and the reflected M-H plot revealed a saturation magnetization of 10 emu/g (Fig. 4b). However, the measured magnetization was six-fold less than the saturation magnetization of magnetite nanoparticles (60 emu/g). Such reduced magnetization is attributable to the mass of the calcium alginate matrix and similar reduced magnetization has been reported earlier [32].

The developed FMCABs exhibited excellent adsorption property for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ ions at pH 6 in aqueous medium as compared to Co2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni 2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Pd2+ and Mg2+. The quantitative adsorption data corresponded to adsorption at equilibrium condition, where the concentrations of the respective metal ions were taken arbitrarily as 1 mg/L for a fixed adsorbent mass of 7.5 g/L. Compared to FMCABs, the adsorption of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by magnetic calcium alginate beads (i.e., without CdS quantum dots) were found to be significantly less, while the adsorption profiles of Pb2+ and Cr3+ were unchanged. From these results it may be remarked that CdS quantum dots played an important role in enhancing the adsorption of Hg2+ and Cu2+, more likely to be due to their affinity for sulphides (in CdS nanoparticles) owing to the HSAB rule [33]. The detailed studies on enhanced adsorption of Hg2+ by FMCABs as discuss below.

pH plays a vital role in adsorption phenomena [34], which is reflected by the maximum adsorption capacity at respective pH. The maximum adsorption capacity (Qe) of FMCAB was studied over a pH range between 3 and 7 ( Fig. 6). The adsorption capacity increased with pH (up to pH 6) and the (Qe) was measured using following expression:
Q
e
=
C
o
−
C
e
m
× V
Where C
o and C
e represent the concentrations of Hg2+ ions in mg/L at t = 0 and at equilibrium time respectively; m is the mass of adsorbent (in g) and V is the volume of solution (in L). Here, the adsorbent-adsorbate system was equilibrated for a larger contact time (t = 2 h). This pH dependency of the adsorption profile could be attributed to the surface charge of FMCAB owing to functional groups like hydroxyl groups (OH-) and the carboxyl groups (COOH-). The functional groups tend to protonate at lower pH and thus adsorption capacity of metals as cations were decreased [35]. Further at lower pH, the Hg2+ ions are likely to exist as hydrated species, which are larger in size than the micro-pores of the calcium alginate adsorbent and hence the adsorption was less. At pH 6, all the functional groups in microbeads were apparently deprotonated and consequently the Hg2+ ions were electrostatically bound with their respective groups. On the other hand, at pH 7 and above pH 7, the adsorption capacity for Hg2+ ions were decreased, which could be due to unstable nature of the polymeric matrix of calcium alginate [36]. The pH dependency observed here was in good agreement with the cases reported for adsorption of Cr3+ by calcium alginate microbeads as an adsorbent [37].

With the increase in the initial concentration of Hg2+ from 105 mg/L to 205 mg/L the adsorption capacity (Qe) of the FMCABs was increased from 9.36 mg/g to 22.21 mg/g, for an adsorbent dose of 7.5 g/L and at an operating pH of 6.0 ( Fig. 8). The Qe of FMCAB increased rapidly with time and was saturated in 30 min for all the batches of Hg2+ ion concentrations, i.e., 105 mg/L to 205 mg/L, which is attributable to enhanced probability of interaction between adsorbate with the surface of the adsorbent. This can be explained on the basis of reduction in the mass transfer resistance at higher concentrations of Hg2+ ions in the solution.

Compare to adsorption of metal ions in microbeads incorporated with IO ( Fig. 9) and microbeads incorporated with IO and CdS, it was noted that significant improvement in adsorption occurred for Hg2+ and Cu2+ (nearly 2 fold increase in adsorption capacity for both the metal ions) it was also noted that adsorption capacity of lead, palladium and chromium was very high in both the batches of adsorbents i.e., the role of CdS was not failed in the case of Pb2+, Pd2+and Cr3+ and contrastically the adsorption capacity of Co2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni 2+, Ag+, and Ba2+ were not responsive to CdS incorporated in the FMCAB.

From this study, it can be concluded that the as-developed fluorescent magnetic beads can be used as an adsorbent for removing toxic heavy metals, e.g., Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, etc. in the presence of each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching due to adsorption of Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence spectroscopy of FMCABs after treating with increasing concentrations of Hg2+ ions revealed gradual fluorescence quenching ( Fig. 10). The fluorescence emission is due to recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes of CdS quantum dots and the quenching behaviour is attributable to inhibition in such recombination of excitons. The fluorescence quenching can be attributed to heavy atom effect of Hg2+ [11]. Further, the dependence of fluorescence quenching of FMCABs on the concentration of analyte (i.e. Hg2+) can be evaluate by characteristic Stern Vomer’s (S-V) relation, expressed as:
Io/I = 1 + Ksv [Hg2+]

where, I
o and I are the fluorescence emission intensities of the pristine FMCABs (can be termed as probe) and FMCABs treated with Hg2+ ions, respectively; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer fluorescence quenching constant, which correspond the sensitivity of the probe to the analyte as quencher, and [Hg2+] is the concentration of mercuric ions. Fig. 11 shows the stern volmer plot showed nonlinear behavior of fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ ions which indicates that the quenching has complex mechanism including both static as well as dynamic quenching processes. The inset of Fig. 11 shows that the plot becomes linear within the range of 2–25 uM, suggesting that dynamic quenching predominates in this particular range.

The adsorbed Hg2+ ions on FMCAB could be desorbed by acidic thiourea (2 M HCl and 2 % (w/v) thiourea). The desorption equilibrium could be achieved in 30 min contact time. Thiourea was essential as the Hg2+ ions upon desorbing from the surface of FMCABs tend to re-adsorb, while presence of thiourea helped in inhibiting re-adsorption phenomenon. Under optimized condition, the adsorption of Hg2+ for five successive cycles was found of the order of 81 – 95 %. The improvement in the adsorption profile in the 3rd cycle onwards ( Fig. 12) could be attributed to surface modification of the FMCABs due to treatment with thiourea during desorption. The thiol group in the thiourea could extend greater affinity for Hg2+ in accordance with HSAB rule.

The adsorption capacity of FMCABs as adsorbent studied at optimized conditions for different initial concentrations of Hg2+ ions is given in Fig. 13. The adsorption capacity increased with initial concentration of Hg2+ ions which is attributable to enhanced probability of interaction between adsorbate with the surface of the adsorbent. This can be explained on the basis of reduction in the mass transfer resistance at higher concentrations of Hg2+ ions in the solution. The adsorption data were better linearly fitted with Langmuir model (R2 = 0.997) for the all the batches of Hg2+ ion concentrations ranging between 105 and 205 mg/L (Fig. 13). The rate constants determined in our studies are better than various types of microbeads as well as conventional adsorbents for Hg2+ ions. These results indicated that the rate determining step of adsorption of Hg2+ in FMCABs was chemisorption and was in good agreement with reported literature [42].

Thermodynamic consideration of an adsorption process gives an insight about its spontaneous nature and indicates about a possible underlying physio-chemical mechanism. The change in the Gibbs free energy (∆G°) indicates the spontaneity of the adsorption process, when ∆G° is a negative quantity. The (∆G°) of an adsorption process is related to sorption distribution co-efficient (Kd) by Van’t Hoff equation:
ΔG o = -RT lnK
d

where K
d is given as concentration of Hg2+ in adsorbent phase /concentration of Hg2+ in the solution at equilibrium condition; R is the ideal gas constant and T is the working temperature. The ∆G values for different temperatures are given in Table 2. Further, K
d is related to change in enthalpy (∆H) and change in entropy (∆S) by Van’t Hoff correlation:
ln
K
d
=
(
Δ S
R
)
−
(
Δ H
R T
)
For the given Hg2+ ion adsorption condition, the ∆H and ∆S values were derived from the slope and the intercept of the plot ln Kd versus 1/T, and are given in Table 2.

Notably, the ∆G° values for the chosen temperature range, i.e., 288 K to 318 K, were negative. It implied that the adsorption of Hg2+ ions on FMCABs was spontaneous in nature. Moreover, the spontaneity increased with temperature from 288 K to 298 K. On the other hand, the plot of lnKd versus 1/T revealed two profiles corresponding temperature range of 288 K to 298 K and 298 K to 318 K. In the lower temperature region, the ∆H value was positive and ∆S value was negative. This indicated that adsorption of Hg2+ on FMCABs was endothermic in nature in the temperature range of 288 K – 298 K. On the other hand the ∆H value was negative in the temperature range of 298 K to 318 K, indicating that the adsorption was exothermic at elevated temperature. This can be explained by taking into consideration of the two opposing processes involved in the solid –liquid system during adsorption, i.e., (a) desorption of pre-adsorbed water molecules (solvent) from the adsorbate surface, and (b) adsorption of Hg2+ ions on the adsorbent. At lower temperature, energy was required by Hg2+ ions to displace water molecules for adsorption and hence exhibited endothermic property. On the other hand, beyond 298 K, the ∆H was negative indicating that energy was released due to adsorption of Hg2+ ions. In this case the total energy absorbed in bond breaking was less than the total energy released in bond making between Hg2+ and the FMCABs and the excess energy was released. The magnitude of ∆H is an important indicator for describing the type of sorption. The adsorption is considered as a physisorption process when the ∆H is less than 20 kJ/mol, while it is a chemisorption process if the ∆H is greater than 80 kJ/mol. In our case of adsorption of Hg2+ on FMCABs, the physico-chemical processes were found to be dependent on temperature. Though it is not a common phenomenon to observe endothermic as well as exothermic behaviour over a small temperature region, but it may be remarked that the adsorption of Hg2+ at normal temperature or higher was an exothermic process and governed by physisorption. This also implied that the interaction of Hg2+ with FMCABs was primarily due to van der waal’s forces or by electrostatic interaction. This can be attributed to electrostatic interaction between Hg2+ and the sulphur group of capped CdS nanoparticles which were decorated on the FMCABs.

Where distribution coefficient K
d is the fraction of amount of Hg2+ ions adsorbed by FMCABs to the amount of metal ions remaining in the solution. Its value can be determined from experimental parameters as given below:
K
d
=
(
C
o
−
C
e
) V
C
e
M
Where Co (initial metal concentration in mg/L) Ce (equilibrium metal concentration in mg/L), Ce (metal concentration at particular time in mg/L), V (volume of the metal solution in mL) and M (the mass of the adsorbent in g)

In conclusion, the magnetically functionalized calcium alginate nanocomposite decorated with cadmium sulfide (CdS) quantum dots synthesized in this study demonstrates significant potential for both metal removal and optical sensing applications, particularly for mercury (Hg(II)) ions. The comprehensive characterization of the nanocomposite through various techniques, including UV–vis absorption, fluorescence emission, XRD, VSM, SEM-EDX, and TEM, confirms its robust structure and functional properties. The material's adsorption performance is notably influenced by pH, with optimal removal of Hg(II) occurring at pH 6. The adsorption kinetics and isotherm analyses indicate that the process is best described by the pseudo-second order kinetic model and Langmuir isotherm, with a maximum uptake capacity of 22.2 mg/g. The rapid achievement of adsorption equilibrium within 30 minutes underscores the efficiency of this material. Furthermore, the ability to regenerate and recycle the adsorbent up to five cycles without significant loss of performance highlights its practicality for long-term use in environmental applications. The stability of the nanocomposite over a year further supports its viability as a durable adsorbent. Additionally, the CdS quantum dots within the nanocomposite provide a valuable optical sensing capability, with detectable changes in optical properties in the presence of Hg(II) ions, making this material a versatile tool for both removal and detection of mercury in aqueous environments. This dual functionality, combined with its recyclability and stability, positions the nanocomposite as a promising candidate for addressing mercury contamination in various settings.
NA.
NA.
NA.
NA.

Ambika Kumar: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Data curation. Nancy Jaswal: Writing – review & editing. Monika: Review & Editing. R.K. Dutta: Validation, Conceptualization, Supervision. Pramod Kumar: Supervision.
